stem: A patient on allopurinol develops painful oral erosions, widespread targetoid skin lesions with epidermal detachment involving 15% BSA. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! SJS: 1-10% BSA epidermal detachment + mucosal involvement (≥2 mucosal sites). SJS/TEN overlap: 10-30% BSA. TEN: >30% BSA. This patient at 15% is SJS/TEN overlap. Nikolsky sign positive (epidermal shearing with lateral pressure). Common drug triggers: allopurinol, sulfonamides, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine), NSAIDs (piroxicam). Treatment: stop offending drug immediately, burn unit/ICU care, wound care, ophthalmology consult (ocular scarring), IV fluids. Role of IVIG, cyclosporine — debated. Mortality: SJS ~5%, TEN ~30%.
Bullous pemphigoid causes tense blisters (subepidermal) primarily in elderly. Nikolsky sign is negative. Not related to drug exposure in this manner.
EM involves <10% BSA with typical target lesions but minimal mucosal involvement and no epidermal detachment.
Pemphigus vulgaris causes flaccid blisters from intraepidermal acantholysis with + Nikolsky sign but is autoimmune (anti-desmoglein antibodies), not drug-induced, and develops gradually.

auditNote: Batch 24: Verified accurate

stem: Which HLA allele testing should be performed before prescribing carbamazepine to prevent SJS/TEN?

choices.feedback: HLA-DQ2 and DQ8 are associated with celiac disease, not drug-induced SJS/TEN.

choices.text: HLA-B*1502 in patients of Southeast Asian descent
choices.feedback: Correct! HLA-B*1502 allele: strongly associated with carbamazepine-induced SJS/TEN in Southeast Asian populations (Han Chinese, Thai, Malaysian, Filipino, Indian). Boxed warning: test before starting carbamazepine in at-risk populations. If positive → do not prescribe. Prevalence: 5-15% in Southeast Asians, very rare in Europeans. Also: HLA-A*3101 associated with carbamazepine hypersensitivity in Europeans/Japanese. HLA-B*5801 → allopurinol hypersensitivity (SJS/DRESS) in Korean, Thai, African American populations.
HLA-DR4 is associated with rheumatoid arthritis susceptibility, not drug hypersensitivity.
HLA-B*5701 testing is for abacavir hypersensitivity, not carbamazepine.

auditNote: Batch 24: Verified accurate

stem: A patient develops a rapidly expanding area of painful erythema with a well-defined border on the face. They have fever and leukocytosis. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Erysipelas: superficial cellulitis (upper dermis + lymphatics) caused primarily by Group A Streptococcus. Classic: sharply demarcated, raised, erythematous, painful plaque with peau d'orange texture. Commonly involves face (butterfly distribution) or lower extremities. Systemic symptoms: fever, chills, leukocytosis. Differs from deeper cellulitis (poorly defined borders, no raised edge). Treatment: penicillin or amoxicillin (covers GAS). If deeper cellulitis suspected: add MRSA coverage if indicated.
Contact dermatitis is pruritic, not painful, and does not cause fever or leukocytosis.
Zoster follows a dermatomal distribution with vesicular lesions, not a uniformly erythematous plaque with raised borders.
Angioedema causes painless subcutaneous swelling without erythema and without fever/leukocytosis.

auditNote: Batch 24: Verified accurate

stem: What is the most common causative organism of non-purulent cellulitis?

choices.feedback: S. aureus is more commonly associated with PURULENT skin infections (abscesses, furuncles). Non-purulent cellulitis has a different predominant pathogen.
Pseudomonas cellulitis is uncommon except in specific scenarios: puncture wounds through shoes (plantar), hot tub folliculitis, or immunocompromised.

Correct! Non-purulent cellulitis (no abscess/drainage): predominantly beta-hemolytic streptococci (Group A > B, C, G). Treatment: beta-lactam antibiotics (cephalexin, dicloxacillin, ceftriaxone). MRSA coverage NOT needed unless purulence present. Purulent cellulitis/abscess: S. aureus (often MRSA in community-acquired) → I&D + TMP-SMX, doxycycline, or clindamycin. This purulent vs non-purulent distinction drives antibiotic selection.
E. coli cellulitis is rare. Streptococci are the predominant non-purulent cellulitis pathogens.

explanation: Correct: Beta-hemolytic streptococci (Group A Strep).
auditNote: Batch 24: Verified accurate

stem: What clinical features should prompt concern for necrotizing fasciitis rather than simple cellulitis?

choices.text: Pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus, rapid spread, systemic toxicity, and skin necrosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Red flags for necrotizing fasciitis: pain DISPROPORTIONATE to visible skin changes (most important early sign), rapid progression despite antibiotics, crepitus (gas in tissues — especially Clostridium), skin necrosis/bullae/ecchymosis, systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension). LRINEC score ≥ 6 suggests necrotizing fasciitis. Emergency surgical exploration is both diagnostic AND therapeutic — do NOT delay for imaging. Treatment: emergent wide surgical debridement + broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin + pip-tazo + clindamycin).
Necrotizing fasciitis progresses rapidly (hours to days), not slowly. Slow progression suggests other diagnoses.
Well-demarcated borders with mild tenderness is more typical of erysipelas or simple cellulitis. Necrotizing fasciitis has different features.
Pruritus and scaling suggest tinea (fungal infection) or eczema, not necrotizing fasciitis.

auditNote: Batch 24: Verified accurate

stem: What is the antibiotic regimen for suspected necrotizing fasciitis?

choices.feedback: Metronidazole covers anaerobes only. Necrotizing fasciitis requires broad coverage for gram-positives, gram-negatives, and anaerobes, plus surgery.
While this covers gram-positives and gram-negatives, it lacks clindamycin's crucial anti-toxin effect and has no anaerobic coverage from aztreonam.

Correct! Triple antibiotic coverage: Vancomycin (MRSA), piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem (gram-negatives + anaerobes), Clindamycin (anti-toxin effect — suppresses toxin production by GAS and S. aureus). SURGERY is the definitive treatment — antibiotics alone are insufficient. Repeat debridement (planned second-look) at 24-48 hours is standard. Mortality: 20-40% even with treatment; higher with delayed surgery.
Necrotizing fasciitis is a surgical emergency requiring IV antibiotics. Oral cephalexin is inadequate.

explanation: Key detail: tazobactam + clindamycin, PLUS emergent surgical debridement.
auditNote: Batch 24: Verified accurate

stem: A patient with recurrent lower extremity cellulitis has chronic lymphedema. What is the most effective strategy to reduce recurrences?

choices.feedback: Topical mupirocin is used for nasal MRSA decolonization and impetigo, not for prevention of recurrent cellulitis.

Correct! Recurrent cellulitis (≥3-4 episodes/year): prophylactic antibiotics (penicillin VK 250- or erythromycin if allergic) reduce recurrence by ~50%. PLUS addressing predisposing factors: lymphedema management (compression stockings, elevation, pneumatic pumps), tinea pedis treatment (portal of entry), skin hydration/care, weight management. Interdigital tinea pedis is a common unrecognized entry point — always examine between toes.
Skin excision is not standard for recurrent cellulitis. Prophylactic antibiotics and lymphedema management are the evidence-based approaches.
IV vancomycin prophylaxis is excessive, impractical, and promotes resistance. Oral penicillin prophylaxis is effective and practical.

explanation: Best answer: Prophylactic oral penicillin and lymphedema management (compression, elevation, skin care).
auditNote: Batch 24: Verified accurate

stem: What is the key clinical distinction between cellulitis and erysipelas?

choices.text: Erysipelas has sharply demarcated, raised borders and involves the superficial dermis/lymphatics
choices.feedback: Correct! Erysipelas: sharply demarcated, raised (indurated), bright red borders, involves upper dermis and superficial lymphatics. Almost exclusively streptococcal. Classically on the face or lower extremities. Cellulitis: deeper (deep dermis/subcutaneous), borders are INDISTINCT and flat, may involve strep or staph. Both treated with beta-lactams; erysipelas may respond to penicillin alone. The sharp border of erysipelas reflects lymphatic involvement creating a visible boundary.

choices.text: Cellulitis always has pus; erysipelas never does
choices.feedback: Purulence depends on pathogen, not the type of infection. Non-purulent cellulitis and erysipelas both typically lack pus.

choices.text: Erysipelas is caused by viruses; cellulitis by bacteria
choices.feedback: Both are bacterial skin infections. Erysipelas is caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci affecting the upper dermis/lymphatics.

choices.text: They are the same condition with different names
choices.feedback: They are distinct entities with different depth of involvement, border characteristics, and slightly different microbiology.

auditNote: Batch 24: Verified accurate

stem: A 34-year-old woman started allopurinol 2 weeks ago. She now presents with fever, malaise, and a rapidly spreading painful erythematous rash with widespread flaccid blisters affecting 18% BSA. Mucous membranes (oral, conjunctival, genital) show hemorrhagic erosions. Nikolsky sign is positive. Skin biopsy shows full-thickness epidermal necrosis.Vital SignsTemp 39.2°CHR 118BP 96/60 What is the diagnosis and most critical management?

choices.text: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome — treat with IV antibiotics
Stevens-Johnson syndrome/TEN overlap (10-30% BSA) — immediately STOP the offending drug (allopurinol), transfer to burn
Erythema multiforme major — self-limited condition requiring only supportive care

explanation:
Correct Answer: SJS/TEN is a life-threatening drug hypersensitivity reaction classified by BSA of epidermal detachment: SJS 30%. #1 priority: STOP the causative drug immediately — every hour of continued exposure increases mortality. Common culprits: allopurinol, sulfonamides, carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenytoin, NSAIDs (piroxicam). Nikolsky sign (lateral pressure on skin causes epidermal separation) = positive. Full-thickness epidermal necrosis on biopsy (vs SSSS which is superficial cleavage at granular layer). Transfer to burn unit. Mortality: SJS 1-5%, TEN 25-35%. SCORTEN predicts mortality.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Allopurinol started 2 weeks ago = common SJS/TEN culprit (1-8 week lag typical)
- 18% BSA detachment = SJS-TEN overlap (10-30%)
- Mucous membrane involvement (oral + conjunctival + genital) = characteristic of SJS/TEN
- Positive Nikolsky sign = epidermal fragility from keratinocyte death
- Full-thickness necrosis = SJS/TEN (SSSS cleaves superficially at granular layer)
- Immediate drug discontinuation is the SINGLE most important intervention

Evidence: SJS/TEN - StatPearls - Frantz R, et al. StatPearls. NBK459323. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- SSSS: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome (SSSS) causes superficial blistering from exfoliative toxins (Nikolsky positive) but: (1) occurs primarily in children <5, (2) does NOT involve mucous membranes, (3) biopsy shows cleavage at the GRANULAR layer (not full-thickness necrosis), (4) is treated with antibiotics. This adult with mucous membrane involvement and full-thickness necrosis has SJS/TEN.
- Erythema multiforme major: EM is typically caused by INFECTIONS (HSV is #1, not drugs), shows classic raised TARGET lesions (3 concentric zones), and has limited mucosal involvement. SJS/TEN is drug-induced, shows flat atypical targets or diffuse erythema with detachment, and has severe multi-mucosal involvement.
- Start high-dose systemic corticosteroids: Systemic corticosteroids for SJS/TEN are CONTROVERSIAL. While early pulse steroids may have some benefit, they increase infection risk and may delay wound healing. More importantly, the #1 intervention is STOPPING THE DRUG — no immunosuppressive therapy can substitute for drug discontinuation.
- Pemphigus vulgaris: Pemphigus vulgaris (PV) causes flaccid blisters with positive Nikolsky sign (overlapping with SJS/TEN), but PV is autoimmune (anti-desmoglein antibodies), NOT drug-induced. PV shows intraepidermal cleavage (not full-thickness necrosis) and does not have the acute drug-temporal relationship seen here.

Reference: SJS/TEN - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459323/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 52-year-old fair-skinned man with a history of multiple blistering sunburns as a teenager presents for evaluation of a mole on his upper back that his wife noticed has been changing. The lesion is 8 mm in diameter, has irregular borders with notching, shows multiple colors (dark brown, black, and a reddish-blue area), and is asymmetric. One area appears raised compared to the previously flat mole. What features of this lesion are most concerning, and what is the next step?

choices.text: Benign compound nevus — normal mole evolution with age
Suspected melanoma meeting multiple ABCDE criteria (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter
Seborrheic keratosis — common benign lesion in older adults
Dysplastic nevus — reassure and monitor with photo documentation
Punch biopsy of the darkest area for pathology

explanation:
Correct Answer: ABCDE criteria for melanoma: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation (especially ≥3 colors), Diameter >6mm, Evolving (any change in size, shape, color, or elevation). This lesion meets ALL five criteria. The critical next step is EXCISIONAL biopsy (full thickness, with 1-3mm margins) — NOT shave biopsy (may not capture Breslow depth, the most important prognostic factor) and NOT incisional/punch of only one area (may miss the thickest part). Breslow thickness determines staging, prognosis, and need for sentinel lymph node biopsy.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Asymmetry ✓, Border irregularity ✓, Color variation (brown/black/reddish-blue) ✓, Diameter 8mm (>6mm) ✓, Evolving (newly raised area) ✓
- Multiple blistering sunburns = major melanoma risk factor
- Fair skin = Fitzpatrick type I-II = higher risk
- Upper back = common site for melanoma in men
- Full-thickness excisional biopsy is MANDATORY to determine Breslow depth
- Shave biopsy may transect the base and underestimate depth = staging error

Evidence: Melanoma - StatPearls - Davis LE, et al. StatPearls. NBK459141. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Benign compound nevus: Benign nevi are symmetric, have regular borders, uniform color, and are stable. This lesion meets all 5 ABCDE criteria for melanoma. Any mole that is changing (evolving) warrants biopsy.
- Seborrheic keratosis: Seborrheic keratoses are waxy, stuck-on appearing lesions with horn cysts and a well-demarcated border. They do NOT show the color variation (reddish-blue areas), irregular borders with notching, or evolution described here.
- Dysplastic nevus — reassure and monitor: While dysplastic nevi share some features (irregular borders, color variation), the degree of atypia (5/5 ABCDE criteria, reddish-blue component, new elevation) requires BIOPSY, not monitoring. A dysplastic nevus may be a melanoma precursor or may harbor melanoma within it.
- Punch biopsy of the darkest area: Partial biopsy (punch or shave) of a suspected melanoma is INADEQUATE because: (1) the thickest area may not be where the darkest area is, (2) Breslow depth measurement requires the ENTIRE lesion, (3) sampling error may miss melanoma within a dysplastic nevus. Excisional biopsy is the standard.

Reference: Melanoma - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459141/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with chronic relapsing pruritic eczematous patches on the antecubital and popliteal fossae (flexural surfaces). He has had this since infancy (starting on the face as an infant, then migrating to flexural areas). Family history includes asthma (mother) and allergic rhinitis (father). Exam shows lichenification, dry skin (xerosis), and Dennie-Morgan folds (infraorbital creases). What is the diagnosis, and what classic triad is this part of?

choices.text: Contact dermatitis — from exposure to an environmental allergen
Atopic dermatitis — chronic relapsing eczema on FLEXURAL surfaces, part of the ATOPIC TRIAD (atopic
Seborrheic dermatitis — greasy scale on scalp and face
Scabies — pruritic rash in a child with family involvement

explanation:
Correct Answer: Atopic dermatitis (eczema): chronic relapsing pruritic dermatosis. Key features: (1) Age-dependent distribution: infants = face/extensors; children = FLEXURAL (antecubital/popliteal); adults = hands/face/flexural. (2) Atopic triad: atopic dermatitis + asthma + allergic rhinitis (shared Th2-mediated pathophysiology). (3) Family history of atopy. (4) Physical findings: xerosis (dry skin), lichenification (thickened skin from chronic scratching), Dennie-Morgan folds. Filaggrin gene mutations underlie the epidermal barrier dysfunction.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Chronic relapsing pruritic rash since infancy = atopic dermatitis timeline
- Flexural distribution (antecubital/popliteal fossae) = classic childhood AD pattern
- Family history: mother with asthma + father with allergic rhinitis = atopic family
- Lichenification = chronic scratching (itch-scratch cycle)
- Dennie-Morgan folds = associated physical finding in atopic individuals
- Xerosis = epidermal barrier dysfunction (filaggrin deficiency)

Evidence: Atopic Dermatitis - StatPearls - Nnoruka EN, et al. StatPearls. NBK448071. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Contact dermatitis: Contact dermatitis is localized to the area of allergen/irritant CONTACT (e.g., nickel from jewelry → earlobe dermatitis; poison ivy → linear streaks). It does NOT have the chronic relapsing flexural distribution, does not start in infancy, and is not associated with the atopic triad.
- Psoriasis: Psoriasis shows well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery SCALE on EXTENSOR surfaces (elbows, knees) and scalp — opposite of AD's flexural distribution. Psoriasis shows Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding on scale removal). There is no atopic family history or flexural predominance.
- Seborrheic dermatitis: Seborrheic dermatitis causes greasy yellowish scale on the scalp, nasolabial folds, eyebrows, and retroauricular areas. It is NOT pruritic in the same way as AD, does not cause flexural lichenification, and is associated with Malassezia yeast (not Th2/IgE dysregulation).
- Scabies: Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei) causes intense pruritus, especially at night, with burrows in web spaces, wrists, genitalia. It is CONTAGIOUS (family members affected simultaneously) and does NOT have the chronic relapsing course from infancy with flexural distribution seen in AD.

Reference: Atopic Dermatitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448071/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 30-year-old woman with no history of atopy presents with a pruritic, erythematous, vesicular rash ONLY on the left wrist in the exact distribution of her new nickel-containing watchband. She started wearing the watch 10 days ago. No rash elsewhere on her body. The parent case showed chronic flexural eczema with atopic family history = AD. How does this localized pattern change the diagnosis?

choices.text: Atopic dermatitis — eczematous rash on the wrist
Allergic contact dermatitis — localized to the EXACT area of nickel contact, geometric borders
Irritant contact dermatitis — all contact reactions are irritant
Cellulitis — erythema and warmth of the wrist
Psoriasis — plaque on the wrist (Koebner phenomenon)

explanation:
Correct Answer: THE PIVOT: AD = chronic, flexural, atopic history, widespread. Contact dermatitis = LOCALIZED to the exact contact area, geometric/linear borders, temporal relationship to allergen exposure. Allergic contact dermatitis (ACD) is a Type IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity reaction — T cell-mediated, occurs 24-72 hours after re-exposure to a sensitized allergen. Nickel is the #1 contact allergen worldwide. Diagnosis confirmed by PATCH testing (applies allergens to back for 48h, reads at 48h and 96h) — NOT skin prick testing (which tests Type I/IgE-mediated reactions).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Localized to left wrist in watchband distribution = contact pattern
- New nickel watch 10 days ago = temporal relationship (sensitization takes 10-14 days on first exposure)
- No history of atopy, no rash elsewhere = not AD
- Geometric borders matching the object = hallmark of contact dermatitis
- Type IV hypersensitivity = T cell-mediated, delayed (vs Type I = IgE, immediate)
- Patch testing confirms specific allergen

Evidence: Contact Dermatitis - StatPearls - Litchman G, et al. StatPearls. NBK532866. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Atopic dermatitis: AD is chronic, flexural, starts in infancy, and has atopic family history. This patient has no atopic history, the rash is localized to ONE area matching an external object, and it appeared for the first time at age 30 after a specific exposure.
- Irritant contact dermatitis (ICD): ICD occurs from direct chemical/physical damage and does NOT require prior sensitization. ICD typically causes more burning than itching and occurs on FIRST exposure. ACD requires prior sensitization (this patient has been wearing the watch for 10 days before rash appeared = sensitization phase) and is more pruritic with vesicles.
- Cellulitis: Cellulitis causes diffuse spreading erythema, warmth, and tenderness from bacterial skin infection. It does NOT cause vesicles, does NOT have geometric borders matching an external object, and is typically accompanied by fever and leukocytosis.
- Psoriasis (Koebner phenomenon): While psoriasis can appear at sites of trauma (Koebner phenomenon), it shows well-demarcated silvery SCALE plaques, not vesicular eczematous dermatitis. Psoriasis at one site would typically be accompanied by plaques elsewhere (scalp, elbows, knees).

Reference: Contact Dermatitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532866/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 35-year-old man presents with well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with thick silvery-white SCALE on bilateral elbows, knees, and scalp. Removal of scale reveals pinpoint bleeding (Auspitz sign). He also has pitting of multiple fingernails. He reports joint pain in his right knee and several DIP joints of his hands. What is the diagnosis, and what complication does the joint involvement suggest?

choices.text: Atopic dermatitis — chronic eczematous skin disease
Psoriasis with psoriatic arthritis — well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale on EXTENSOR surfaces,
Seborrheic dermatitis — scaly plaques on the scalp
Rheumatoid arthritis with dermatitis — joint pain with skin findings
Secondary syphilis — scaly plaques on palms and soles

explanation:
Correct Answer: Psoriasis: well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale on EXTENSOR surfaces (elbows, knees) and scalp (opposite of AD which is flexural). Auspitz sign: pinpoint bleeding when scale is removed (exposing dilated capillaries in elongated dermal papillae). Nail changes: pitting, oil-drop sign, onycholysis, subungual hyperkeratosis. Psoriatic arthritis (PsA): occurs in 30% of psoriasis patients. PsA patterns: DIP predominant, asymmetric oligoarthritis, symmetric polyarthritis (RA-like), spondylitis, arthritis mutilans. PsA is seronegative (RF and anti-CCP negative) — distinguishes from RA.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale = classic psoriatic morphology
- Extensor surfaces (elbows, knees) + scalp = psoriasis distribution
- Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding on scale removal) = pathognomonic
- Nail pitting = psoriatic nail disease (70% of PsA patients)
- DIP joint pain = DIP-predominant PsA pattern (RA spares DIPs)
- Seronegative spondyloarthropathy = part of HLA-B27-associated spectrum

Evidence: Psoriasis - StatPearls - Nair PA, et al. StatPearls. NBK448194. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Atopic dermatitis: AD affects FLEXURAL surfaces (antecubital/popliteal fossae), shows eczematous patches (not well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale), and does NOT cause Auspitz sign or nail pitting. AD is on the opposite side of the extremities from psoriasis.
- Seborrheic dermatitis: Seborrheic dermatitis causes greasy yellowish scale on the scalp, nasolabial folds, and eyebrows. While it can overlap with scalp psoriasis (sebopsoriasis), it does NOT cause silvery plaques on elbows/knees, Auspitz sign, or nail pitting.
- Rheumatoid arthritis with dermatitis: RA does NOT affect DIP joints (it affects MCPs, PIPs, wrists). RA is seropositive (RF+, anti-CCP+). PsA is seronegative and has DIP predominance, dactylitis, and enthesitis — features absent in RA. The skin findings are not consistent with any RA-associated dermatosis.
- Secondary syphilis: Secondary syphilis causes diffuse maculopapular rash including palms and soles, condylomata lata, and mucous patches. While it can have scaly lesions, it does NOT cause the well-demarcated silvery plaques on extensors with Auspitz sign. RPR/VDRL screening differentiates.

Reference: Psoriasis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448194/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of raised, pruritic, erythematous wheals (hives) occurring almost daily for the past 8 weeks. Individual lesions resolve within 24 hours without scarring, but new ones appear. She has no identified trigger. She also has intermittent lip and periorbital swelling (angioedema). Labs: CBC, TSH, CRP are normal. She takes no ACE inhibitors. What is the classification, workup, and first-line treatment?

choices.text: Acute urticaria — antihistamines and allergen avoidance
Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) — defined as urticaria lasting ≥6 WEEKS;
Hereditary angioedema — check C4 and C1 esterase inhibitor

explanation:
Correct Answer: Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU): urticaria lasting ≥6 weeks, occurring most days, without an identifiable external trigger. Key diagnostic feature: individual wheals resolve within 24h, leave bruising/scarring → biopsy if suspected). 80-90% of CSU is IDIOPATHIC (autoimmune: IgG autoantibodies against FcεRI receptor or IgE itself → mast cell degranulation). Treatment ladder: Step 1: 2nd-gen H1-antihistamine (cetirizine 10mg daily). Step 2: UPDOSE to 2-4x standard (cetirizine 20-40mg daily — safe per guidelines). Step 3: Add OMALIZUMAB (anti-IgE mAb, 300mg SC q4 weeks). Step 4: Add cyclosporine. ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema: always ask about medications — ACEi causes bradykinin-mediated angioedema (no urticaria, not histamine-mediated).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 8 weeks of daily urticaria = chronic (≥6 weeks meets definition)
- Individual wheals <24 hours, no scarring = CSU (NOT urticarial vasculitis)
- No identified trigger = spontaneous (not inducible)
- Angioedema accompanies urticaria in 40-50% of CSU
- No ACE inhibitor use = rules out ACEi angioedema
- Normal labs = supports idiopathic/autoimmune mechanism

Evidence: Chronic Urticaria - StatPearls - Bernstein JA, et al. StatPearls. NBK555910. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Acute urticaria: Acute urticaria lasts <6 weeks. This patient has had daily hives for 8 weeks (≥6 weeks = chronic by definition). Acute urticaria is more commonly triggered by identifiable allergens (foods, medications, infections), while CSU is typically idiopathic.
- Hereditary angioedema (HAE): HAE causes angioedema WITHOUT urticaria (no hives, no pruritus). HAE is bradykinin-mediated (not histamine), C4 is low, C1-INH is low or dysfunctional, and it does NOT respond to antihistamines or epinephrine. This patient has urticaria (wheals) WITH angioedema = histamine-mediated CSU, not HAE.
- Allergic contact dermatitis: Contact dermatitis causes localized eczematous lesions (papulovesicles, not wheals) in the pattern of contact with the allergen, and individual lesions persist for days-weeks (not resolving within 24 hours). This patient has migratory wheals consistent with urticaria, not the localized eczematous pattern of contact dermatitis.
- Systemic mastocytosis: Mastocytosis should be considered if there are systemic symptoms (flushing, syncope, diarrhea, bone pain) or serum tryptase is persistently elevated. This patient has normal labs and no systemic symptoms beyond skin findings. Bone marrow biopsy is reserved for suspected systemic mastocytosis with elevated tryptase.

Reference: Chronic Urticaria - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK555910/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 52-year-old woman with diabetes and obesity presents with 3 days of progressive redness, warmth, swelling, and pain of the right lower leg. She had a minor abrasion on the right shin 5 days ago. She has a fever. There is a well-demarcated area of bright erythema, warmth, and tenderness over the anterior right lower leg with advancing borders marked by pen. Interdigital tinea pedis is present.Vital SignsTemp 38.6°CHR 94ExamRight leg Well-demarcated bright erythema with warmth and tenderness; advancing border; no crepitus; no fluctuanceSkin break Healing abrasion on anterior shinFeet Interdigital tinea pedis (athlete's foot) with macerationLeft leg NormalLabsWBC 14,200Blood_cultures Pending What is the diagnosis, what is the most common portal of entry, and what is first-line treatment?

choices.text: Deep vein thrombosis — order D-dimer and compression ultrasound
Cellulitis — a non-purulent skin infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue;
Erysipelas — a more superficial infection confined to the upper dermis
Stasis dermatitis — bilateral lower extremity erythema from venous insufficiency

explanation:
Correct Answer: Cellulitis: non-purulent infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue. Most common portal of entry: TINEA PEDIS (interdigital maceration breaks the skin barrier, allowing bacterial entry; treating tinea reduces cellulitis recurrence by ~50%). Other portals: abrasions, ulcers, surgical sites, insect bites. Most common organisms: Group A Streptococcus (most common) and S. aureus. First-line for non-purulent cellulitis: cephalexin 500mg QID or dicloxacillin 500mg QID (covers GAS + MSSA). If purulent/abscess component or MRSA risk: add TMP-SMX or doxycycline. Cellulitis vs DVT: cellulitis has FEVER, well-demarcated erythema, warmth, and a portal of entry. DVT has unilateral swelling without fever or well-demarcated erythema. Cellulitis vs necrotizing fasciitis: necrotizing fasciitis has pain OUT OF PROPORTION, crepitus, dusky/necrotic skin, rapid spread, and hemodynamic instability.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Unilateral erythema + warmth + tenderness + fever = cellulitis
- Portal of entry identified: healing abrasion + tinea pedis (skin barrier disrupted)
- Well-demarcated advancing border = classic cellulitis (can track with pen)
- Fever (38.6°C) + WBC 14,200 = systemic response to infection
- No crepitus, no fluctuance, no pain out of proportion = NOT necrotizing fasciitis
- Tinea pedis = address this to prevent recurrence

Evidence: Cellulitis - StatPearls - Raff AB, et al. StatPearls. NBK549770. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Deep vein thrombosis: DVT causes unilateral leg swelling, calf pain/tenderness, but does NOT cause well-demarcated erythema, fever, or a portal of entry. DVT edema is diffuse (not well-demarcated advancing erythema). However, DVT and cellulitis can coexist, and DVT should be considered if no portal of entry is found and the patient has risk factors.
- Necrotizing fasciitis: Necrotizing fasciitis causes pain OUT OF PROPORTION to exam findings, crepitus (gas in tissues), rapidly spreading dusky/necrotic skin, hemodynamic instability, and does NOT respond to antibiotics alone. This patient has proportionate pain, no crepitus, no necrosis, and is hemodynamically stable = cellulitis, not necrotizing fasciitis.
- Erysipelas: Erysipelas is a more superficial infection (confined to upper dermis and lymphatics) that is SHARPLY demarcated, RAISED (peau d'orange appearance), and bright red. While related to cellulitis, erysipelas has a more clearly elevated border and is almost exclusively caused by GAS. This patient's infection involves deeper tissue (subcutaneous tenderness) = cellulitis rather than erysipelas.
- Stasis dermatitis: Stasis dermatitis from venous insufficiency causes BILATERAL lower extremity erythema, edema, and skin changes (hemosiderin deposition, lipodermatosclerosis). It is chronic (not acute over 3 days), bilateral, and NOT associated with fever. This patient has unilateral, acute, febrile erythema with a clear portal of entry = infectious, not venous stasis.

Reference: Cellulitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK549770/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with painful oral erosions that have been present for 3 months, making eating difficult. Over the past 4 weeks, she has developed flaccid blisters on her trunk and upper arms that rupture easily, leaving painful erosions that do not heal. There are no intact bullae currently visible — only raw erosions with overlying crusts.ExamOral Multiple painful erosions on buccal mucosa, palate, and gingivaSkin Widespread erosions with overlying crusts on trunk and proximal extremities; no intact bullaeNikolsky sign POSITIVE (gentle lateral pressure on normal-appearing skin causes superficial shearing)Skin biopsy (H&E) Suprabasal acantholysis (intraepidermal blister formation just above the basal layer)DIF Net-like (reticular) pattern of IgG and C3 deposition in INTERCELLULAR SPACES of the epidermis ("chicken wire" or "fishnet" pattern) What is the diagnosis, and what antibodies are responsible?

choices.text: Bullous pemphigoid — tense blisters in elderly, subepidermal
Pemphigus vulgaris (PV) — autoantibodies (IgG) against DESMOGLEIN 3 (mucosal) and desmoglein 1 (mucocutaneous);
Oral lichen planus — white reticular pattern (Wickham striae)
Dermatitis herpetiformis — intensely pruritic grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces

explanation:
Correct Answer: Pemphigus vulgaris vs bullous pemphigoid is a critical dermatology differential. PV: flaccid blisters (intraepidermal, fragile), positive Nikolsky sign, ORAL INVOLVEMENT (50-70% starts in mouth), suprabasal acantholysis, DIF: IgG in intercellular spaces ("chicken wire"), anti-desmoglein 1 and 3 antibodies. BP: tense blisters (subepidermal, thicker roof), negative Nikolsky, NO oral involvement typically, subepidermal split, DIF: linear IgG at basement membrane zone, anti-BP180/BP230 antibodies. Mnemonic: PV is intra-epidermal (Pemphigus = Peeling apart keratinocytes); BP is sub-epidermal (Bullous Pemphigoid = Below epidermis).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Painful oral erosions for 3 months preceding skin lesions = PV (oral first in 50-70%)
- Flaccid blisters that rupture easily leaving erosions = intraepidermal (thin roof, fragile)
- Positive Nikolsky sign = loss of intercellular adhesion (acantholysis)
- Suprabasal acantholysis on biopsy = PV (separation just above basal layer)
- DIF: intercellular IgG (chicken wire/fishnet) = diagnostic for pemphigus
- Anti-desmoglein 3 (mucosal predominance) and anti-desmoglein 1 (skin involvement)

Evidence: Pemphigus Vulgaris - StatPearls - Bystryn JC, et al. StatPearls. NBK560860. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Bullous pemphigoid (BP): BP causes TENSE blisters (subepidermal, thick roof that doesn't rupture as easily), NEGATIVE Nikolsky sign, typically NO oral involvement, and DIF shows LINEAR IgG at the basement membrane zone (not intercellular). BP occurs in elderly (>70), unlike PV (40-60). BP antibodies target BP180/BP230 (hemidesmosomal proteins at the basement membrane), causing a subepidermal split.
- Stevens-Johnson syndrome: SJS is a drug-induced mucocutaneous reaction with target lesions, full-thickness epidermal necrosis, and mucous membrane erosions (overlap with PV). However, SJS has an acute onset (1-3 weeks after drug exposure), full-thickness necrosis (not suprabasal acantholysis), and no intercellular IgG on DIF. SJS is NOT autoimmune.
- Oral lichen planus: Oral lichen planus causes white reticular patches (Wickham striae), erosive mucositis, but does NOT cause flaccid skin blisters, positive Nikolsky sign, or intercellular IgG on DIF. Lichen planus on biopsy shows a band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermoepidermal junction with civatte bodies (apoptotic keratinocytes).
- Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH): DH causes intensely PRURITIC (not painful) grouped vesicles on EXTENSOR surfaces (elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp) associated with celiac disease. DIF shows GRANULAR IgA at the dermal papillae (not intercellular IgG). DH responds to dapsone and gluten-free diet. It does NOT cause oral erosions or positive Nikolsky sign.

Reference: Pemphigus Vulgaris - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560860/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 68-year-old fair-skinned man with a history of extensive sun exposure presents with a slowly enlarging lesion on his nose that has been present for approximately 8 months. It occasionally bleeds when he washes his face. Exam shows a 1.2 cm pearly, translucent papule with rolled borders and visible telangiectasias on the left nasal ala. There is a small central ulceration.ExamLesion 1.2 cm pearly papule, rolled borders, telangiectasias, central ulcerationLocation Left nasal alaRegional LN None palpable What is the most likely diagnosis, and what distinguishes it from squamous cell carcinoma?

choices.text: Squamous cell carcinoma — hyperkeratotic, scaly plaque arising from actinic keratosis
Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) — the MOST COMMON cancer in humans;
Melanoma — pigmented lesion with ABCDE criteria
Actinic keratosis — precancerous rough scaly patch
Sebaceous hyperplasia — benign yellowish papules common on the face

explanation:
Correct Answer: BCC vs SCC: BCC = most common cancer in humans (80% of skin cancers). Classic: pearly/translucent papule with rolled borders, telangiectasias, central ulceration. Locally invasive, almost NEVER metastasizes (<0.1%). SCC: hyperkeratotic, scaly plaque/nodule, often arising from actinic keratosis (precursor), CAN metastasize (2-6%). Both share UV radiation as the primary risk factor. BCC subtypes: nodular (most common, classic pearly papule), superficial (thin, erythematous, trunk), morpheaform/infiltrative (scar-like, poorly defined, most aggressive locally). Treatment: Mohs surgery (face, cosmetically sensitive, aggressive subtypes); excision with margins; electrodesiccation and curettage (superficial); topical 5-FU or imiquimod (superficial BCC only).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Pearly translucent papule = classic BCC appearance (waxy, shiny)
- Rolled borders = raised, curled edges characteristic of nodular BCC
- Telangiectasias visible on surface = dilated blood vessels feeding tumor
- Central ulceration = "rodent ulcer" from outward tumor growth with central necrosis
- Nose/face = most common BCC location (sun-exposed, highest UV dose)
- Slow growth over 8 months = typical for BCC (SCC often grows faster)

Evidence: BCC - StatPearls - Cameron MC, et al. StatPearls. NBK482439. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Squamous cell carcinoma: SCC typically presents as a hyperkeratotic (crusty, scaly) plaque or nodule, often arising from an actinic keratosis precursor. SCC lacks the pearly translucent quality, rolled borders, and telangiectasias of BCC. SCC is more firm/indurated. The key clinical distinction: BCC = pearly + rolled borders + telangiectasias; SCC = scaly + keratotic + ulcerated.
- Melanoma: Melanoma is a pigmented lesion (brown/black, though amelanotic forms exist) evaluated by ABCDE criteria. This pearly, translucent, non-pigmented papule with telangiectasias is classic for BCC, not melanoma. Melanoma requires excisional biopsy with margins determined by Breslow depth.
- Actinic keratosis: Actinic keratoses are PRE-cancerous rough, scaly patches on sun-damaged skin. They are flat or slightly raised, gritty/sandpaper texture, and erythematous with adherent scale. They lack the pearly quality, rolled borders, and telangiectasias of BCC. AKs are precursors to SCC (not BCC).
- Sebaceous hyperplasia: Sebaceous hyperplasia presents as small (1-3mm) yellowish papules with a central dell (umbilication) on the forehead/cheeks. They can mimic BCC but are softer, yellowish (not pearly), and lack telangiectasias and ulceration. Dermoscopy distinguishes them (crown vessels vs arborizing vessels).

Reference: BCC - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482439/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with 3 days of burning pain along the left T4 dermatome followed by grouped vesicles on an erythematous base that appeared yesterday. The vesicles are distributed in a band along the left chest wall and DO NOT cross the midline. She has not received the zoster vaccine.ExamRash Grouped vesicles on erythematous base in T4 dermatomal distributionDistribution Unilateral, does NOT cross midlinePain Severe burning, 8/10Hutchinson sign Absent (no vesicles on nose tip) What is the treatment, and when must it be started to be effective?

choices.text: Topical acyclovir cream is adequate treatment
Herpes zoster (shingles) — antiviral therapy (valacyclovir 1g TID or acyclovir 800mg 5x/day) must be started
Antibiotics for bacterial cellulitis — the erythema suggests infection
Wait for lesions to resolve on their own without treatment

explanation:
Correct Answer: Herpes zoster (shingles): reactivation of latent VZV in dorsal root ganglia → dermatomal vesicular eruption. CRITICAL 72-HOUR WINDOW: antiviral therapy must be started within 72 hours of rash onset to be maximally effective. Valacyclovir 1g TID x 7 days (preferred, better bioavailability) or acyclovir 800mg 5x/day x 7 days. Even after 72 hours, antivirals may still benefit patients with ongoing new vesicle formation. Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN): persistent pain ≥90 days after rash; occurs in 10-18% overall, up to 30-50% in >60 years; most debilitating complication. PHN treatment: gabapentin, pregabalin, tricyclics (amitriptyline), lidocaine patch, capsaicin patch. Hutchinson sign: vesicles on the nose tip = nasociliary branch (V1) involvement → indicates herpes zoster ophthalmicus → EMERGENT ophthalmology referral (corneal involvement risk). Shingrix vaccine: recombinant, 2-dose series, >90% efficacy, recommended for ALL adults ≥50.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Grouped vesicles on erythematous base in dermatomal distribution = classic zoster
- Unilateral, does NOT cross midline = follows single dermatome (T4)
- Prodromal burning pain 3 days before rash = typical (pain precedes rash by 2-4 days)
- 24 hours since rash onset = within the critical 72-hour treatment window
- 72-year-old = high risk for PHN (age is the strongest risk factor)
- No Hutchinson sign = no nasociliary involvement (no emergent ophthalmology referral needed)

Evidence: Herpes Zoster - StatPearls - Patil A, et al. StatPearls. NBK441824. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Topical acyclovir: Topical acyclovir is INEFFECTIVE for herpes zoster. The virus is replicating in the dorsal root ganglion and along the sensory nerve — topical therapy cannot achieve adequate tissue levels at these sites. SYSTEMIC antiviral therapy (oral valacyclovir or acyclovir) is required.
- Antibiotics for cellulitis: This is NOT cellulitis. Cellulitis causes diffuse, spreading, poorly demarcated erythema without vesicles and does NOT follow a dermatomal distribution. The grouped vesicles on erythematous base in a unilateral dermatomal band is pathognomonic for herpes zoster. Secondary bacterial superinfection can occur but is treated in addition to, not instead of, antivirals.
- Wait without treatment: Untreated zoster in a 72-year-old carries a 30-50% risk of PHN, which can cause debilitating pain lasting months to years. Starting antivirals within 72 hours reduces PHN incidence by ~50% and shortens disease duration. Not treating a high-risk patient is a missed opportunity to prevent significant morbidity.
- Systemic corticosteroids alone: Corticosteroids WITHOUT antivirals are inappropriate. Steroids may reduce acute inflammation and pain but do NOT inhibit viral replication and do NOT prevent PHN. They are sometimes used as an ADJUNCT to antivirals (not instead of) in selected patients with severe pain and no contraindications.

Reference: Herpes Zoster - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441824/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 32-year-old woman started allopurinol 2 weeks ago for gout. She now presents with fever, malaise, and a painful rash that began on her trunk and spread to her face and extremities. She has mucosal involvement (oral erosions, conjunctival injection). Skin shows targetoid lesions with central blistering and areas of epidermal detachment.Vital SignsTemp 39.1°CHR 108BP 98/62ExamSkin Targetoid macules with central necrosis, positive Nikolsky sign, ~15% BSA detachmentMucosa Oral erosions, hemorrhagic crusting of lips, bilateral conjunctival injection with pseudomembrane formationGenitalia Erosions present What is the diagnosis, severity classification, and management?

choices.text: Erythema multiforme minor — supportive care only
Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS) — this is a DERMATOLOGIC EMERGENCY:
Drug reaction — continue allopurinol and add antihistamines

explanation:
Correct Answer: SJS/TEN spectrum:
• SJS: SJS-TEN overlap: 10-30% BSA
• TEN: >30% BSA

High-risk drugs: Allopurinol (#1), sulfonamides, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine), NSAIDs, nevirapine, antibiotics.

SCORTEN (mortality prediction): age >40, malignancy, BSA >10%, HR >120, BUN >28, glucose >252, bicarb 35% mortality.

Nikolsky sign: lateral pressure on skin causes epidermal separation (also positive in pemphigus, SSSS).

Critical: Ophthalmology consult within 24h — ocular involvement causes blindness if untreated.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Allopurinol started 2 weeks ago = typical latency (1-8 weeks)
- Fever + malaise + painful rash = prodrome
- Targetoid lesions with central necrosis = SJS pattern
- Mucosal involvement (oral + ocular + genital) = required for SJS diagnosis
- 15% BSA detachment = SJS-TEN overlap
- Nikolsky positive = epidermal fragility
- STOP allopurinol immediately
- Burn unit/ICU transfer
- Urgent ophthalmology

Evidence: SJS/TEN - StatPearls - Harr T, et al. StatPearls. NBK459323. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Erythema multiforme minor: EM minor has targetoid lesions but is typically triggered by HSV (not drugs), affects <10% BSA, and has MINIMAL or NO mucosal involvement. This patient has extensive mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital) and 15% detachment — this is SJS-TEN overlap, not EM.
- Pemphigus vulgaris: Pemphigus causes flaccid blisters and erosions with positive Nikolsky sign, but it is an AUTOIMMUNE disease (anti-desmoglein antibodies), not drug-induced. Pemphigus lacks the acute febrile prodrome, targetoid morphology, and drug exposure history. Biopsy would show acantholysis.
- Continue allopurinol: NEVER continue the offending drug in SJS/TEN. Each additional day of exposure increases mortality. Allopurinol must be stopped immediately and the patient should NEVER be re-exposed. Alternative urate-lowering therapy (febuxostat) can be considered after recovery.
- Staphylococcal scalded skin: SSSS is caused by S. aureus exfoliative toxins and affects CHILDREN (<5 years typically). It causes superficial blistering (cleavage plane in granular layer) WITHOUT mucosal involvement. This adult with drug exposure and mucosal erosions has SJS, not SSSS.

Reference: SJS/TEN - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459323/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old man with fair skin and history of multiple sunburns presents with a pigmented lesion on his upper back that his wife noticed has changed over the past 6 months. The lesion is 9mm in diameter with irregular borders and variable coloration (brown, black, and red areas). One edge appears slightly raised.ExamLesion 9mm pigmented macule/papule with asymmetry, irregular notched borders, color variegation (brown, black, red), diameter >6mmOther Multiple atypical nevi on trunk, fair skin, blue eyes What features raise concern, and what is the next step?

choices.text: Suspected melanoma — this lesion meets ABCDE criteria:
Atypical nevus — photograph and follow in 3 months
Dermoscopy alone is sufficient for diagnosis

explanation:
Correct Answer: ABCDE criteria for melanoma:
• Asymmetry — one half unlike the other
• Border — irregular, notched, scalloped
• Color — variable (brown, black, red, white, blue)
• Diameter — >6mm (though smaller melanomas exist)
• Evolution — changing in size, shape, color, or symptoms

Ugly duckling sign: a nevus that looks different from the patient's other nevi.

Biopsy technique: EXCISIONAL with 1-2mm margins preferred to assess full depth (Breslow thickness is the most important prognostic factor). Shave biopsy may transect the lesion and underestimate depth.

Risk factors: fair skin, UV exposure, sunburns, family history, many nevi (>50), atypical nevi, immunosuppression.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Asymmetry = A criterion
- Irregular notched borders = B criterion
- Brown + black + red = C criterion (color variation)
- 9mm = D criterion (>6mm)
- Changed over 6 months = E criterion (evolution)
- Fair skin + sunburns + atypical nevi = high-risk patient
- Excisional biopsy to assess Breslow depth

Evidence: Melanoma - StatPearls - Davis LE, et al. StatPearls. NBK470409. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Seborrheic keratosis: Seborrheic keratoses are benign, 'stuck-on' appearing, waxy lesions that are common in older adults. They do NOT show color variation with red components, irregular borders, or rapid evolution. This lesion has multiple concerning features for melanoma.
- Observe in 3 months: A lesion meeting multiple ABCDE criteria should NOT be observed — it requires biopsy NOW. Delay allows potential melanoma to grow deeper, worsening prognosis. Observation is appropriate only for clearly benign lesions or very mildly atypical nevi.
- Shave biopsy: Shave biopsy may transect the lesion and make it impossible to assess Breslow depth, which is the MOST important prognostic factor. For pigmented lesions suspicious for melanoma, EXCISIONAL biopsy with narrow margins is the correct technique.
- Dermoscopy alone: Dermoscopy improves diagnostic accuracy but does NOT replace histologic diagnosis. Even experienced dermatologists cannot diagnose melanoma definitively by dermoscopy — biopsy is required to confirm diagnosis and assess depth. Dermoscopy helps decide which lesions to biopsy.

Reference: Melanoma - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470409/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a painful, swollen, erythematous area on his left lower leg after a minor scrape 3 days ago. The area is warm, tender, and spreading. There is no fluctuance or purulent drainage. He is afebrile with no systemic symptoms.Vital SignsTemp 37.4°CHR 82BP 128/78ExamSkin 10cm area of erythema, warmth, tenderness on left shin; no fluctuance, no drainage; advancing border marked with penLymph Inguinal lymphadenopathy What is the diagnosis and appropriate antibiotic choice?

choices.text: Non-purulent CELLULITIS — infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue
No antibiotics needed — warm compresses only

explanation:
Correct Answer: Cellulitis classification determines treatment:

| Type | Features | Pathogen | Treatment |
|------|----------|----------|----------|
| Non-purulent | Erythema, warmth, no drainage | Strep (GAS) | Cephalexin, dicloxacillin |
| Purulent | Abscess, drainage, fluctuance | MRSA | I&D + TMP-SMX or doxy |

Key clinical distinction: palpate for fluctuance. No fluctuance + no purulent drainage = non-purulent (treat for strep). Fluctuance or drainage = purulent (I&D + MRSA coverage).

Red flags for necrotizing fasciitis: pain out of proportion, crepitus, rapid spread, bullae, systemic toxicity, skin necrosis.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Erythema + warmth + tenderness = cellulitis
- No fluctuance, no drainage = non-purulent
- Afebrile, no systemic symptoms = mild, outpatient manageable
- Strep is most common cause of non-purulent cellulitis
- Cephalexin covers strep
- Mark border to monitor response
- MRSA coverage NOT needed for non-purulent cellulitis

Evidence: Cellulitis - StatPearls - Stevens DL, et al. StatPearls. NBK549770. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Abscess requiring I&D: There is NO fluctuance and NO purulent drainage — this is non-purulent cellulitis, not an abscess. I&D is only indicated when there is a drainable fluid collection. Palpation should assess for fluctuance; if uncertain, ultrasound can help.
- Necrotizing fasciitis: Necrotizing fasciitis presents with pain OUT OF PROPORTION to exam findings, rapid progression, crepitus, bullae, skin necrosis, and systemic toxicity (high fever, tachycardia, hypotension). This patient has mild cellulitis without concerning features. Nec fasc is a surgical emergency.
- IV vancomycin: IV vancomycin for MRSA coverage is NOT needed for non-purulent cellulitis. Multiple RCTs show no benefit of adding MRSA coverage to non-purulent cellulitis treatment. MRSA causes abscesses; strep causes spreading cellulitis. This patient needs oral cephalexin, not IV vancomycin.
- Warm compresses only: Cellulitis is a bacterial infection requiring antibiotics. Warm compresses may provide symptomatic relief but will not treat the infection. Untreated cellulitis can progress to bacteremia, abscess formation, or necrotizing infection.

Reference: Cellulitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK549770/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 45-year-old man presents with fever and a widespread maculopapular rash 10 days after starting allopurinol for gout. He has facial swelling, tender lymphadenopathy, and labs show eosinophilia (18%), elevated AST/ALT (186/224), and creatinine 1.8 (baseline 1.0).Vital SignsTemp 38.6°CHR 92ExamSkin Widespread morbilliform eruption, facial edemaLymph Diffuse lymphadenopathyLiver Tender hepatomegaly What syndrome is this, and what distinguishes it from simple drug rash?
explanation:
Correct Answer: DRESS vs simple drug rash vs SJS:
• Simple rash: no systemic features, no eosinophilia
• DRESS: morbilliform + facial edema + eosinophilia + organ involvement
• SJS/TEN: targetoid lesions + MUCOSAL involvement + skin detachment

DRESS drugs: Dapsone, Allopurinol, Anticonvulsants, Sulfonamides.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Rash 10 days after allopurinol = drug-induced
- Morbilliform + facial edema = DRESS pattern
- Eosinophilia 18% = key criterion
- Elevated AST/ALT + elevated Cr = organ involvement
- STOP drug immediately, systemic steroids

Evidence: DRESS Syndrome - StatPearls - Shiohara T, et al. StatPearls. NBK459320. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Simple drug eruption: No eosinophilia, no organ involvement in simple drug rash.
- Viral exanthem: No eosinophilia, no drug temporal relationship.
- SJS: SJS has targetoid lesions, mucosal involvement, skin detachment — not morbilliform with eosinophilia.
- Scarlet fever: Scarlet fever follows strep pharyngitis with sandpaper rash, not drug-triggered eosinophilia.

Reference: DRESS - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459320/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with 3 days of fever, malaise, and a painful rash that started on her trunk and is spreading. She was started on allopurinol 3 weeks ago. She has painful erosions in her mouth and both eyes are red with discharge.Vital SignsTemp 39.0°CHR 108ExamSkin Widespread erythematous macules with central dusky necrosis, some with flaccid blisters; positive Nikolsky sign; BSA involvement approximately 15%Mucous_membranes Oral erosions, hemorrhagic lip crusting, bilateral conjunctival injection with pseudomembrane formation What is this diagnosis, and how does BSA involvement classify severity?

choices.text: DRESS syndrome — stop drug and start steroids
Pemphigus vulgaris — start systemic steroids and rituximab
Erythema multiforme major — self-limited, supportive care only
Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome — IV nafcillin

explanation:
Correct Answer: SJS/TEN spectrum classified by BSA of epidermal detachment: SJS (30%). This patient at 15% = overlap. STOP the offending drug immediately (most important step). Mucosal involvement in ≥2 sites (oral + ocular here) is characteristic. Nikolsky sign positive (lateral pressure causes skin to slough). High-risk drugs: allopurinol, anticonvulsants (carbamazepine, phenytoin, lamotrigine), sulfonamides, NSAIDs (oxicam), antibiotics (fluoroquinolones). Management: burn center transfer, aggressive fluid/electrolyte replacement, wound care (similar to burns), ophthalmology consultation (corneal scarring risk), nutrition support. SCORTEN score predicts mortality. Role of steroids is controversial. Cyclosporine may be beneficial if given early.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Drug rash 3 weeks after allopurinol = drug-induced
- Targetoid lesions with central necrosis = SJS/TEN spectrum
- Positive Nikolsky sign = epidermal detachment
- BSA 15% = SJS-TEN overlap
- Oral + ocular mucosal involvement = 2 mucosal sites
- STOP allopurinol immediately
- Burn center transfer for specialized wound care
- Ophthalmology for corneal protection

Evidence: SJS/TEN - StatPearls - Harr T, et al. StatPearls. NBK459323. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- DRESS: DRESS has morbilliform rash with EOSINOPHILIA and organ involvement (hepatitis, nephritis) but does NOT have epidermal detachment, Nikolsky sign, or mucosal erosions. Facial edema is characteristic of DRESS, while skin sloughing is characteristic of SJS/TEN.
- Pemphigus vulgaris: Pemphigus has a positive Nikolsky sign and oral erosions, but is an autoimmune blistering disease (anti-desmoglein antibodies) with a chronic course, NOT a drug reaction. It lacks the targetoid lesions and acute drug-temporal relationship.
- Erythema multiforme major: EM major has targetoid lesions and can involve mucosa, but has distinct clinical 'target' morphology (3 concentric zones), is often triggered by HSV (not drugs), and does NOT progress to skin sloughing. BSA involvement is less extensive.
- SSSS: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome has a positive Nikolsky sign but occurs in CHILDREN (<5 years), is caused by staphylococcal exfoliative toxins (not drugs), has superficial blistering (NOT full-thickness), and does NOT involve mucous membranes. Adults with SSSS-like picture have SJS/TEN until proven otherwise.

Reference: SJS/TEN - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459323/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 48-year-old diabetic woman presents with a warm, erythematous, tender, expanding area on her right lower leg without a drainable abscess. She has mild tinea pedis between her toes. Temp 38.1°C. No crepitus, no bullae, no dusky discoloration. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

choices.text: Oral cephalexin and treat the tinea pedis

explanation:
Correct Answer: Non-purulent cellulitis (no abscess): treat with beta-lactam targeting beta-hemolytic streptococci (cephalexin, dicloxacillin). MRSA coverage NOT routinely needed for non-purulent cellulitis. Mark borders to track progression. Treat predisposing conditions (tinea pedis is the most common portal of entry for lower extremity cellulitis — disrupted skin barrier between toes).

Evidence: Cellulitis - StatPearls - Raff AB, Kroshinsky D. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK549770. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- IV vancomycin: Reserved for severe cellulitis with systemic toxicity or failed oral therapy. This is a mild-moderate case.
- I&D: No drainable abscess. I&D indicated only for purulent collections.
- Clindamycin for MRSA: MRSA coverage NOT recommended for non-purulent cellulitis. Streptococcus is the primary pathogen.
- Topical mupirocin: Cannot penetrate to the level of infection in cellulitis. Systemic antibiotics required.

stem: A 35-year-old man presents with well-demarcated, salmon-colored plaques with thick silvery scale on bilateral elbows, knees, and scalp. He also reports stiffness in several finger joints for the past 3 months. Nails show pitting and oil-drop discoloration. Which of the following is the most appropriate comprehensive management approach?

choices.text: Refer to dermatology and rheumatology for systemic therapy evaluation

explanation:
Correct Answer: This patient has plaque psoriasis with psoriatic arthritis (PsA): joint stiffness + nail changes (pitting, oil-drop sign = pathognomonic for psoriatic nail disease). PsA occurs in 30% of psoriasis patients. Requires systemic therapy — topicals alone are insufficient. Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-17i, IL-23i) treat both skin and joint disease. Oral steroids are contraindicated in psoriasis (rebound flare, risk of pustular transformation).

Evidence: Psoriasis - StatPearls - Nair PA, Badri T. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK448194. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Topicals only: Insufficient for psoriatic arthritis. Topicals treat skin but not joint disease. Untreated PsA leads to joint destruction.
- Oral prednisone: CONTRAINDICATED in psoriasis. Can trigger severe rebound flare or life-threatening generalized pustular psoriasis upon taper.
- Phototherapy alone: Effective for skin disease but does not treat PsA.
- Topical calcineurin inhibitors: Useful for face/intertriginous areas but insufficient for widespread plaques and joints.

Reference: Psoriasis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448194/ [Accessed 2025]

explanation:
Correct Answer: Most common drug reaction (75-95%). Onset 7-14 days. No mucosal involvement. Self-limited.

stem: An 82-year-old woman presents with tense, fluid-filled blisters on her trunk and extremities for 2 weeks. The blisters are on erythematous bases and do not rupture easily. Nikolsky sign is negative. She has intense pruritus. Skin biopsy with DIF shows linear IgG and C3 at the dermal-epidermal junction.Vital SignsBP 136/82HR 78RR 16Temp 98.6°FSpO₂ 98% Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

choices.text: Superpotent topical corticosteroid (clobetasol) to affected areas

explanation:
Correct Answer: For bullous pemphigoid, superpotent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol) are first-line treatment. The landmark French study showed topical clobetasol was more effective and safer than systemic prednisone, especially in elderly patients.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Topical clobetasol was superior to oral prednisone in the Joly et al. trial (NEJM 2002)
- Systemic steroids carry significant morbidity in elderly (infections, fractures, hyperglycemia)
- Nikolsky-negative tense blisters + linear IgG/C3 at BMZ = classic bullous pemphigoid
- Steroid-sparing agents (doxycycline, dapsone, MMF) for maintenance

Evidence: Bullous Pemphigoid - StatPearls - Kershenovich R, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK535374. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Systemic prednisone: Effective but inferior to topical clobetasol and carries significant morbidity in elderly patients.
- Rituximab: Reserved for refractory cases; not first-line for new-onset BP.
- Dapsone: Steroid-sparing adjunct; not standard monotherapy for initial treatment.
- IVIG: Reserved for severe/refractory cases, not initial therapy.

Reference: Bullous Pemphigoid - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK535374/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 45-year-old man presents with an asymmetric, irregularly bordered, multicolored 8 mm lesion on his upper back. Shave biopsy confirms malignant melanoma with Breslow thickness 1.2 mm, no ulceration, mitotic rate 2/mm².Vital SignsBP 128/76HR 72RR 14Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the workup?

choices.text: Wide local excision with 1 cm margins only
Wide local excision with sentinel lymph node biopsy
Observation with serial dermoscopy every 3 months

explanation:
Correct Answer: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is recommended for melanomas with Breslow thickness ≥0.8 mm. SLNB provides critical staging information and guides adjuvant therapy decisions.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- SLNB recommended for Breslow ≥0.8 mm (NCCN guidelines)
- 1.2 mm thickness → WLE with 1-2 cm margins + SLNB
- Imaging (CT, PET) reserved for stage III-IV or symptomatic patients
- SLNB-positive patients may benefit from adjuvant immunotherapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab)

Evidence: Melanoma - StatPearls - Switzer B, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK459367. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- CT staging: Imaging is not routinely recommended for stage I-II melanoma without symptoms or positive SLNB.
- WLE only without SLNB: 1.2 mm thickness exceeds the 0.8 mm threshold for SLNB recommendation.
- PET-CT: Not recommended for initial staging of clinically localized melanoma.
- Observation only: Biopsy-confirmed melanoma requires definitive surgical management, not observation.

Reference: Melanoma - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459367/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a well-circumscribed, smooth, round patch of complete hair loss on her scalp that she noticed 3 weeks ago. There is no scaling, scarring, or erythema. The borders show short, broken "exclamation point" hairs. She has no other medical conditions.Vital SignsBP 118/72HR 68RR 14Temp 98.2°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most appropriate initial workup?

choices.text: No workup needed — clinical diagnosis is sufficient

explanation:
Correct Answer: The presentation is classic for alopecia areata (smooth, non-scarring patch with exclamation point hairs). Initial workup includes TSH, CBC, and ferritin to screen for associated autoimmune thyroid disease and contributing nutritional deficiencies, which are more common in AA patients.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Alopecia areata is associated with autoimmune thyroid disease (8-28% of patients)
- Exclamation point hairs are pathognomonic for alopecia areata
- No scaling or broken hairs with scale rules out tinea capitis (which would need KOH)
- Biopsy reserved for diagnostic uncertainty, not classic presentations

Evidence: Alopecia Areata - StatPearls - Lepe K, Zito PM. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK537000. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Scalp biopsy with DIF: Not needed for classic presentation; biopsy reserved for atypical or scarring alopecia.
- KOH and fungal culture: Tinea capitis causes scaling, broken hairs, and possible kerion — absent here.
- ANA panel: SLE can cause alopecia but presentation here is classic AA, not lupus. ANA not first-line.
- No workup: While diagnosis is clinical, screening for associated thyroid disease is recommended.

Reference: Alopecia Areata - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537000/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 65-year-old fair-skinned man who worked as a lifeguard for 30 years presents with a pearly, translucent papule with telangiectasias on his nose. It has been slowly growing for 6 months and occasionally bleeds with minor trauma.Vital SignsBP 132/78HR 70RR 14Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Dermoscopic monitoring with photos every 3 months
Shave or punch biopsy of the lesion

explanation:
Correct Answer: A pearly papule with telangiectasias on the nose of a sun-exposed patient is highly suspicious for basal cell carcinoma. Tissue biopsy is required to confirm the diagnosis and determine histologic subtype before definitive treatment.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Biopsy confirms diagnosis and identifies subtype (nodular, superficial, morpheaform/infiltrative)
- Subtype guides treatment: superficial may use topical therapy; morpheaform requires Mohs
- Cannot treat without tissue diagnosis — empiric cryotherapy is inappropriate
- Mohs is definitive treatment for nasal BCC but requires histologic confirmation first

Evidence: Basal Cell Carcinoma - StatPearls - Cameron MC, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK482439. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Dermoscopic monitoring: Suspicious lesion requires biopsy, not monitoring. Delay risks continued growth and tissue destruction.
- Empiric cryotherapy: Tissue diagnosis is needed before treatment; cryotherapy also destroys tissue for future histologic evaluation.
- Immediate Mohs: Mohs requires confirmed tissue diagnosis and surgical planning; biopsy comes first.
- Topical imiquimod: Only indicated for superficial BCC subtype — cannot determine subtype without biopsy.

Reference: Basal Cell Carcinoma - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482439/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a photosensitive malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds, oral ulcers, and arthritis for 3 months. She also has a discoid plaque on her scalp with follicular plugging and scarring alopecia. ANA is positive at 1:640.Vital SignsBP 122/74HR 82RR 16Temp 98.8°FSpO₂ 98% In addition to anti-dsDNA and complement levels, which of the following workup components is most important for characterizing her skin disease?

choices.text: Skin biopsy of the discoid lesion with direct immunofluorescence
Skin prick testing for drug allergy

explanation:
Correct Answer: Skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence (DIF) of the discoid lesion is essential. DIF shows the "lupus band" (granular IgG, IgM, C3 deposits at the dermal-epidermal junction), confirming cutaneous lupus and distinguishing from mimics.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- DIF of lesional skin shows immune complex deposition at the dermal-epidermal junction (lupus band)
- Discoid lupus can occur with or without systemic lupus — biopsy helps characterize and prognosticate
- H&E shows interface dermatitis, follicular plugging, basement membrane thickening
- Discoid LE with scarring has risk of progression to SLE in ~5-10% of cases

Evidence: Discoid Lupus Erythematosus - StatPearls - Hejazi EZ, Werth VP. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK493145. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Patch testing: Tests for contact allergens, not relevant to lupus photosensitivity.
- Skin prick testing: For IgE-mediated allergies, not autoimmune skin disease.
- Serum tryptase: Relevant for mastocytosis/anaphylaxis, not lupus skin disease.
- Wood lamp only: Useful for pigmentary disorders and tinea but does not provide tissue diagnosis.

Reference: Discoid Lupus Erythematosus - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK493145/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with a scaly, pruritic patch on his scalp with broken-off hairs ("black dot" pattern). There is cervical lymphadenopathy. His younger sister was recently treated for a similar scalp condition.Vital SignsBP 100/64HR 88RR 18Temp 99.0°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?

choices.text: KOH preparation of hair and scale

explanation:
Correct Answer: KOH preparation of infected hair and scale is the appropriate first-line diagnostic test for suspected tinea capitis. It demonstrates fungal hyphae and spores, confirming the diagnosis. Fungal culture identifies the species but takes weeks.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- KOH shows arthroconidia within (endothrix) or around (ectothrix) the hair shaft
- Trichophyton tonsurans (most common in US) causes endothrix, black dot pattern, no fluorescence
- Fungal culture is gold standard for speciation but requires 2-4 weeks
- Oral antifungals required — topical antifungals do NOT penetrate hair follicle

Evidence: Tinea Capitis - StatPearls - Leung AKC, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK440848. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Scalp biopsy: Invasive and unnecessary when KOH can provide rapid diagnosis.
- Blood cultures: Not a systemic infection; blood cultures are not indicated.
- ANA panel: Lupus can cause alopecia but presentation here is classic tinea capitis.
- Serum IgE: IgE is not relevant to dermatophyte infections.

Reference: Tinea Capitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK440848/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 55-year-old man presents with painful oral erosions for 2 months, now developing flaccid blisters on his trunk that rupture easily leaving raw, denuded areas. Nikolsky sign is positive. He has no history of new medications.Vital SignsBP 126/78HR 84RR 16Temp 99.2°FSpO₂ 98% Which of the following workup findings would confirm the diagnosis?

choices.text: Linear IgA at the dermal-epidermal junction on DIF
Intercellular IgG and C3 in the epidermis on DIF with anti-desmoglein antibodies
Granular IgA in dermal papillae on DIF
Subepidermal blister with eosinophils on H&E

explanation:
Correct Answer: Pemphigus vulgaris is confirmed by DIF showing intercellular ("chicken-wire") IgG and C3 within the epidermis, combined with positive anti-desmoglein 1 and/or 3 antibodies on ELISA. This distinguishes it from other autoimmune blistering diseases.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Flaccid blisters + positive Nikolsky + oral erosions = pemphigus vulgaris
- DIF: intercellular IgG/C3 in epidermis ("chicken-wire" pattern) is pathognomonic
- Anti-desmoglein 3 (mucosal) and anti-desmoglein 1 (mucocutaneous) — titers correlate with activity
- Linear IgA = linear IgA bullous dermatosis; granular IgA in papillae = dermatitis herpetiformis

Evidence: Pemphigus Vulgaris - StatPearls - Porro AM, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK560860. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Linear IgA at DEJ: Linear IgA bullous dermatosis, not pemphigus.
- Granular IgA in dermal papillae: Dermatitis herpetiformis (associated with celiac disease).
- Subepidermal blister with eosinophils: Bullous pemphigoid — subepidermal, not intraepidermal.
- Anti-BP180 antibodies: Diagnostic of bullous pemphigoid, not pemphigus vulgaris.

Reference: Pemphigus Vulgaris - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560860/ [Accessed 2025]
